html:
[808]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 尚了了記前生 - Indicates that the subject could still vaguely remember their past life, suggesting a state of partial or fading memory.
  • 恍惚不甚記 - Means 'vaguely and not very clearly remembered,' describing a state of uncertain or faint recollection.
  • 但記是一歲貢生 - Means 'only remembers being a juren (a scholar who passed the provincial examination),' highlighting a specific detail in the subject's memory.
  • 家去長山不遠 - Means 'the family lived not far from Changshan,' providing a geographical context.
  • 夜中能見物，與晝無異 - Means 'could see things at night, just like during the day,' describing a supernatural ability.
  • 嗜欲日增，則神明日減 - Means 'as desires increase, the clarity of the spirit decreases,' suggesting a philosophical or spiritual insight.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 袁愚谷制府 - Refers to Yuan Yugu, a high-ranking official, with '制府' indicating his position as a governor or viceroy.
  • 長山 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in China.
  • 直隸總督 - The highest-ranking official in the province of Zhili (now part of Hebei Province), a significant administrative position.
  • 諡清愨 - The posthumous title given to Yuan Yugu, indicating his character and achievements.
  • 同硯席 - Refers to being classmates or study partners, emphasizing a close relationship.
  • 姻家 - Means 'in-law' or 'family by marriage,' indicating a familial connection.
  • 一歲貢生 - Refers to a scholar who passed the provincial examination, a significant academic achievement in the imperial examination system.

Proper Nouns

  • 袁愚谷制府 - Yuan Yugu, the high-ranking official.
  • 長山 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in China.
  • 直隸總督 - The highest-ranking official in the province of Zhili.
  • 諡清愨 - The posthumous title given to Yuan Yugu.

[809]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有胆 - Means 'courageous' or 'bold.'
  • 恬不为惧 - Means 'calm and fearless.'
  • 格斗 - Means 'fighting' or 'clashing.'
  • 痴立于旁 - Means 'standing there blankly.'
  • 腼腆良久 - Means 'blushing for a long time.'
  • 妓女 - Refers to a courtesan or prostitute.
  • 妓家之例 - Means 'the custom of courtesans.'
  • 冥途 - Means 'the afterlife' or 'the underworld.'
  • 仍操旧术 - Means 'still practicing the old ways.'
  • 不能一一记姓名 - Means 'unable to remember all the names.'
  • 不敢言谁有约，亦不敢言谁无约也 - Means 'dare not say who has an agreement, nor dare say who does not.'
  • 举首则三鬼皆逝矣 - Means 'when he looked up, the three ghosts had all disappeared.'
  • 闻舅祖陈公 - Means 'hearing from his great-granduncle, Mr. Chen.'
  • 讳颖孙 - Means 'his courtesy name was Ying Sun.'
  • 岁久失记其字号 - Means 'after many years, his titles and names have been forgotten.'
  • 德音公之弟 - Means 'the younger brother of Duke De.'
  • 庚子进士 - Means 'the jiazi year's jinshi (imperial scholar).'
  • 仙居知县秋亭之祖也 - Means 'the ancestor of Xiu Ting, the magistrate of Xianju.'
  • 说亲见一事 - Means 'he spoke about a matter related to marriage.'
  • 亲串中有殁后妾改适者 - Means 'among his relatives, there was someone who remarried after the death of their husband.'
  • 魂附病婢灵语 - Means 'the spirit attached itself to an ill maid and spoke.'
  • 先愦愦 - Means 'previously confused' or 'previously muddled.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 景州 - Refers to Jingzhou, a region in northern China.
  • 李西厓 - The name of the storyteller, Li Xiyi.
  • 鬼语嘈杂 - Refers to the noisy conversation of ghosts.
  • 庚子 - Refers to the jiazi year, which corresponds to the year 1660 in the Gregorian calendar.
  • 德音公 - Refers to Duke De, a noble title.
  • 仙居 - Refers to Xianju, a place name.
  • 秋亭 - Refers to Xiu Ting, a person's name.
  • 说亲 - Refers to the practice of arranging marriages.
  • 亲串 - Refers to relatives or close family connections.

Proper Nouns

  • 景州 - A region in northern China.
  • 李西厓 - The name of the storyteller.
  • 庚子 - The jiazi year, corresponding to 1660 in the Gregorian calendar.
  • 德音公 - A noble title.
  • 仙居 - A place name.
  • 秋亭 - A person's name.
  • 陈公 - A person's name.
  • 颖孙 - A courtesy name.
  • 知县 - A title for a county magistrate.

[810]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 講學者 - Refers to someone engaged in scholarly discussion or debate.
  • 無鬼 - The philosophical position that ghosts or spirits do not exist.
  • 墟墓中獨宿 - Literally 'to sleep alone in a graveyard,' used to test the existence of ghosts.
  • 納涼一夜 - Means 'to cool off for one night,' referring to the act of seeking relief from heat.
  • 毅然竟往 - Means 'firmly and resolutely went,' indicating determination.
  • 果無所見 - Means 'indeed saw nothing,' indicating the absence of ghosts.
  • 歸益自得 - Means 'returned feeling even more self-satisfied,' showing personal satisfaction.
  • 重赍千里 - Means 'to travel a long distance with heavy baggage,' used metaphorically to describe a journey with significant effort.
  • 路不逢盗 - Means 'not encountering bandits on the road,' used to illustrate the absence of danger.
  • 野不遇兽 - Means 'not meeting wild animals in the wild,' used to illustrate the absence of danger.
  • 举一废百 - Means 'to take one example and discard a hundred,' indicating a narrow-minded approach.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 講學者 - Refers to scholars engaged in philosophical debates during the Song Dynasty.
  • 朱文公 - Zhu Xi (1130–1200), a key figure in Neo-Confucianism, known for his commentaries on the *Four Books*.
  • 阮瞻 - Ruan Zhan (254–314), a philosopher from the Jin Dynasty who argued against the existence of ghosts.
  • 二程 - Refers to Cheng Hao and Cheng Yi, brothers who were influential early Neo-Confucian scholars.
  • 魂升魄降 - A concept in Chinese philosophy that describes the movement of the soul and spirit, often discussed in the context of life and death.
  • 灵怪 - Refers to supernatural phenomena, which were often debated in philosophical and religious contexts.
  • 金去伪录 - A text by Jin Quwei, a scholar who commented on the views of the Cheng brothers.

Proper Nouns

  • 講學者 - Scholars engaged in philosophical debates.
  • 朱文公 - Zhu Xi, a prominent Neo-Confucian philosopher.
  • 阮瞻 - Ruan Zhan, a philosopher from the Jin Dynasty.
  • 二程 - Cheng Hao and Cheng Yi, early Neo-Confucian scholars.
  • 金去伪录 - A text by Jin Quwei, a scholar who commented on the views of the Cheng brothers.

[811]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 鬼神之迹也 - Classical expression meaning 'the traces or manifestations of ghosts and spirits,' used metaphorically to refer to natural or supernatural phenomena.
  • 白日公平正直之鬼神 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'ghosts and spirits of fairness and righteousness in daylight,' suggesting benevolent, visible, and just spiritual beings.
  • 有啸于梁，触于胸 - Vivid idiom describing supernatural phenomena such as a ghost howling from the beam or touching the chest, often associated with malevolent or unseen spirits.
  • 不正邪暗 - Compound adjective meaning 'unjust, evil, and obscure,' used to describe malevolent or ambiguous spiritual forces.
  • 或有或无，或来或去，或聚或散 - Series of parallel clauses meaning 'sometimes present, sometimes absent; sometimes coming, sometimes going; sometimes gathering, sometimes dispersing,' used to describe the unpredictable nature of certain spirits.
  • 祷之而应，祈之而获 - Classical expression meaning 'when prayed to, they respond; when asked for, they grant,' used to describe the effectiveness of spiritual intervention through prayer or supplication.
  • 同一理也 - Philosophical phrase meaning 'it is the same principle,' suggesting that both benevolent and malevolent spirits are governed by the same underlying laws or forces.
  • 定不如释家所云，世俗所见也 - Comparative phrase meaning 'definitely not as the Buddhist tradition says, nor as the common people see it,' indicating a rejection of conventional or religious views on the nature of ghosts and spirits.
  • 不可以理推者 - Phrase meaning 'those that cannot be explained by reason or logic,' referring to inexplicable phenomena.
  • 莫要理会 - Colloquial expression meaning 'do not need to concern yourself with it' or 'there's no need to worry about it.'
  • 硬不信 - Colloquial phrase meaning 'refuses to believe, even stubbornly,' used to describe someone who is unwilling to accept evidence of the supernatural.
  • 魑魅尪魉 - Classical term for a variety of supernatural creatures or spirits, often used to refer to malevolent or mischievous entities.
  • 深山大泽 - Phrase meaning 'deep mountains and great lakes,' referring to remote or natural places where spirits are believed to reside.
  • 人往占之，岂不为祟？ - Rhetorical question meaning 'if people go there and occupy it, would it not cause harm or be a source of calamity?' used to warn against encroaching on such places.
  • 豫章刘道人 - Proper noun referring to a Daoist practitioner or hermit from Yuzhang (modern-day Nanchang, Jiangxi), indicating a specific individual with religious or mystical status.
  • 结庵 - Term meaning 'to build a hermitage' or 'to establish a small dwelling for religious practice.'
  • 众蜥蜴入耒 - Phrase meaning 'many lizards entered the耒' (a farming tool or plow), suggesting an unusual or supernatural event.
  • 尽吃庵中水 - Phrase meaning 'they all drank the water in the hermitage,' indicating the lizards consumed the water.
  • 堆雹 - Term meaning 'to pile up hail,' referring to a sudden hailstorm.
  • 妻伯刘大人 - Proper noun referring to a relative or respected elder, likely a maternal uncle or in-law, with the surname Liu.
  • 甚朴实，不能妄语 - Phrase meaning 'very honest and down-to-earth, not prone to lying,' describing a person of integrity.
  • 过一岭，闻溪边林中响 - Phrase meaning 'as he passed a mountain pass, he heard a sound in the forest by the stream,' describing a moment of supernatural or eerie occurrence.
  • 各抱一物，如水晶去 - Phrase meaning 'each holding an object like crystal, moving away,' suggesting the lizards were carrying translucent objects.
  • 未数里下雹 - Phrase meaning 'before a few miles, hail fell,' indicating a sudden and possibly supernatural weather event.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 杨道夫 - Name, likely referring to a scholar or commentator, possibly from the Song or Ming dynasty, who recorded this text.
  • 包扬 - Another name, possibly a scholar or philosopher, who offered a different perspective on the nature of ghosts and spirits.
  • 南轩 - Refers to Ouyang Xiu (also known as 'Nan Xuan'), a prominent scholar and statesman of the Song dynasty, known for his Confucian views and skepticism of the supernatural.
  • 禹鼎 - Reference to the legendary bronze cauldron of Yu the Great, often symbolizing legitimacy or divine authority.
  • 豫章 - Historical name for Jiangxi province, specifically referring to the area around Nanchang.
  • 刘道人 - Daoist practitioner or hermit, suggesting a religious or mystical figure.
  • 结庵 - Term associated with Daoist or Buddhist monastic practice, indicating a person who has retreated from society for spiritual cultivation.
  • 妻伯 - Term referring to a maternal uncle or in-law, used to indicate a respected elder in the family.

Proper Nouns

  • 杨道夫 - Name of a commentator or scholar; to be rendered consistently as 'Yang Daofu.'
  • 包扬 - Name of a commentator or philosopher; to be rendered consistently as 'Bao Yang.'
  • 南轩 - Reference to Ouyang Xiu; to be rendered consistently as 'Nanxuan' or 'Ouyang Xiu' with a note.
  • 禹鼎 - Legendary object; to be rendered consistently as 'Yudi' or 'the cauldron of Yu.'
  • 豫章 - Historical name for Jiangxi; to be rendered consistently as 'Yuzhang.'
  • 刘道人 - Daoist practitioner; to be rendered consistently as 'Liu Dao Ren.'
  • 妻伯刘大人 - Familial or respected figure; to be rendered consistently as 'Uncle Liu' or 'Mr. Liu, the maternal uncle.'

[812]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 此理又不知如何 - Rhetorical expression meaning 'This principle is again unclear' or 'We do not know how this works.'
  • 一方尊信之 - 'One region revered and believed in it.' '一方' refers to a locality or region, and '尊信' means to revere and trust.
  • 佛颈泥木出舍利 - 'Relics (舍利) appeared from the neck of the Buddha made of clay and wood.' A miraculous event common in Buddhist tradition.
  • 泥木岂有此物 - 'Clay and wood could not contain such things.' A rhetorical question emphasizing that the material could not produce the miraculous relics.
  • 只是人心所致 - 'It was merely the result of human hearts.' Attributes the miracle to the collective faith or intention of the people.
  • 因论薛士龙家见鬼 - 'Because he discussed the case of seeing ghosts in the household of Xue Shilong.' Introduces a narrative or anecdote.
  • 世之信鬼神者，皆谓实有，在天地间 - 'Those in the world who believe in ghosts and spirits all say they are real and exist in the world.' General statement about belief in supernatural beings.
  • 其不信者，断然以为无鬼 - 'Those who disbelieve them firmly say there are no ghosts.' Contrast to the previous statement.
  • 然却又有真个见者 - 'Yet there are also those who actually see them.' Introduces a paradox: some disbelieve in ghosts, yet others have genuine experiences.
  • 郑景望遂以薛氏所见为实 - 'Zheng Jingwang then took Xue's sighting as real.' Reference to a person who accepts the supernatural experience as true.
  • 不知此特虹霓之类耳 - 'Not knowing that this is merely a phenomenon like a rainbow.' Clarification that what was seen was not a ghost, but a natural or optical phenomenon.
  • 问虹霓只是气，还有形质 - 'Asking whether a rainbow is only air, or if it has form and substance.' Question about the nature of a rainbow.
  • 既能啜水，亦必有肠肚 - 'If it can drink water, it must also have intestines.' Metaphorical or humorous way of saying that if a phenomenon has some physical action, it must have a physical structure.
  • 只才散便无 - 'But as soon as it disperses, it is gone.' Refers to the ephemeral nature of a rainbow or similar phenomenon.
  • 雷部神亦此类 - 'The thunder gods are of this same kind.' Compares the phenomenon to other supernatural beings that are not solid or permanent.
  • 世之见鬼者甚多，不审有无如何 - 'There are many people in the world who see ghosts, but we do not know whether they exist or not.' Introduces a question about the reality of ghost sightings.
  • 世间人见者极多，如何谓无 - 'Many people in the world have seen them, so how can one say they do not exist?' Rebuttal to the claim that ghosts do not exist.
  • 但非正理耳 - 'But it is not a correct principle.' Qualification that while the sightings are real, they are not based on sound reasoning or truth.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大佛 - A large Buddha statue, likely a wooden or clay image. In Chinese Buddhist tradition, such statues are often revered and may be the subject of miraculous events.
  • 舍利 - 'Relics' or 'sacred remains.' In Buddhism, the relics of the Buddha or great monks are considered sacred and may appear miraculously in connection with a statue or shrine.
  • 薛士龙 - Xue Shilong: A historical or literary figure, possibly from a narrative or anecdotal source, who is said to have seen a ghost.
  • 郑景望 - Zheng Jingwang: Another historical or literary figure who is referenced as believing in the authenticity of Xue's ghost sighting.
  • 虹霓 - A rainbow. In Chinese philosophical and Daoist thought, natural phenomena like rainbows were sometimes interpreted as spiritual or supernatural signs.
  • 雷部神 - 'Thunder gods' or 'gods of thunder.' These are deities in Chinese mythology associated with lightning and storms. They are often depicted as powerful but transient beings.
  • 吴必大录曰 - 'Wu Bidai recorded:...' Wu Bidai is the name of a compiler or recorder of this anecdote. This suggests the text may be part of a larger collection or commentary.
  • 林赐录曰 - 'Lin Ci recorded:...' Lin Ci is another compiler or recorder, indicating that the text may have multiple sources or versions.

Proper Nouns

  • 吴必大 - Wu Bidai: A person who recorded or compiled this text. The name is used in the phrase '吴必大录曰' (Wu Bidai recorded:...).
  • 薛士龙 - Xue Shilong: A person who supposedly saw a ghost.
  • 郑景望 - Zheng Jingwang: A person who believed in the authenticity of Xue Shilong’s ghost sighting.
  • 林赐 - Lin Ci: Another recorder or compiler of this text, referenced in '林赐录曰' (Lin Ci recorded:...).

[813]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 伯有为厉 - Bao You as a ghost, where '厲' (lì) refers to a malevolent spirit or ghost.
  • 气未当尽而强死 - The vital energy had not fully dissipated, but died prematurely.
  • 魂魄无所归 - Soul and spirit have no place to return.
  • 夜行，见无数形像，似人非人 - Traveling at night, seeing countless shapes, resembling humans but not humans.
  • 询之此地，乃昔人战场也 - Inquiring about this place, it turned out to be an ancient battlefield.
  • 死而为厉 - Died and became a ghost.
  • 放爆仗，焚其所依之树 - Set off firecrackers, burned the tree it relied on.
  • 他枉死，气未散，被爆仗惊散 - He died unjustly, his energy had not dissipated, and was scattered by the firecrackers.
  • 沈僴录曰 - Shen Xian recorded that...
  • 坐间或云 - Someone in the audience said...
  • 万人杰录曰 - Wan Renjie recorded that...
  • 死而气散，泯然无迹者，是其常道理 - Died and the energy dispersed, leaving no trace, which is the normal course of things.
  • 恁地有托生者，是偶然聚得气不散 - Those who reincarnate are those who accidentally gathered their energy and did not disperse.
  • 恁生去凑著那生气便再生 - They are reborn by coinciding with the vital energy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 伯有 - Bao You, a historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period, known for his ghostly appearances.
  • 伊川 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location or a person.
  • 淮上 - On the Huai River, referring to a region in China.
  • 战场 - Battlefield, indicating a site of historical conflict.
  • 爆仗 - Firecrackers, a traditional Chinese tool used to ward off evil spirits.
  • 沈僴 - Shen Xian, a historical figure or scholar.
  • 万人杰 - Wan Renjie, another historical figure or scholar.

Proper Nouns

  • 伯有 - Bao You, a historical figure.
  • 伊川 - A place name or person.
  • 淮上 - A region in China.
  • 战场 - Battlefield.
  • 爆仗 - Firecrackers.
  • 沈僴 - Shen Xian, a historical figure or scholar.
  • 万人杰 - Wan Renjie, another historical figure or scholar.

[814]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一件公事 - Refers to a specific official case or matter.
  • 妇杀夫 - Means 'a woman killing her husband,' a specific crime.
  • 密埋之后为祟 - Means 'after secretly burying, causing disturbances,' referring to the deceased causing supernatural disturbances.
  • 事已发觉 - Means 'the matter was discovered.'
  • 当时便不为祟 - Means 'it did not cause disturbances at that time.'
  • 若不与决罪 - Means 'if not punished with a verdict.'
  • 死者之冤必不解 - Means 'the deceased's wrongs will not be resolved.'
  • 或问世有庙食之神 - Means 'someone asked about gods who are worshipped and fed in temples.'
  • 绵历数百年 - Means 'lasting for several centuries.'
  • 浸久亦散 - Means 'over time, they also disperse.'
  • 昔守南康 - Means 'when I was governor of Nankang.'
  • 久旱不免遍祷于神 - Means 'during a long drought, I could not avoid praying to the gods everywhere.'
  • 忽到一庙 - Means 'suddenly came to a temple.'
  • 但有三间敝屋 - Means 'there were only three dilapidated rooms.'
  • 狼藉之甚 - Means 'in a very disordered state.'
  • 彼人言三五十年前 - Means 'the people there said three or five decades ago.'
  • 其灵如响 - Means 'their spirits responded immediately.'
  • 有人来，而帷中之神与之言者 - Means 'when someone came, the deity in the curtain would speak to them.'
  • 昔之灵如彼，今之灵如此 - Means 'their spirit was like that then, and it is like this now.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 潭州 - Refers to the ancient name of Changsha, Hunan Province.
  • 庙食之神 - Refers to deities who are worshipped and fed in temples, often associated with local folklore and historical figures.
  • 南康 - Refers to Nankang, an ancient name for a region in Jiangxi Province.
  • 久旱 - Refers to a prolonged drought, a common natural disaster in ancient China.

Proper Nouns

  • 潭州 - Changsha, Hunan Province.
  • 南康 - Nankang, Jiangxi Province.
  • 李壮祖 - A recorder or historian.

[815]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 論鬼神之事 - Discussing matters concerning ghosts and deities.
  • 灌口二郎廟 - The temple dedicated to the Second Son of Li Bing at Guankou.
  • 開離堆立廟 - Establishment of a temple after opening the Li River levee.
  • 現許多靈怪 - Appearance of many miraculous phenomena.
  • 第二兒子出來 - Manifestation of the second son of Li Bing.
  • 初間封為王 - Initially, he was enfeoffed as a king.
  • 徽宗好道 - Emperor Huizong was fond of Daoism.
  • 遂改封為真君 - Consequently, he was enfeoffed as a True Lord.
  • 張魏公用兵 - Zhang Wei used military force.
  • 禱於廟 - Prayed at the temple.
  • 其夜夢神語曰 - That night, he dreamed that the god spoke.
  • 我向來封為王 - I was previously enfeoffed as a king.
  • 有血食之奉 - Received offerings of meat.
  • 故威福得行 - Therefore, I could exercise power and bestow blessings.
  • 今號為真君 - Now I am called a True Lord.
  • 雖尊 - Although respected.
  • 人以素食祭我 - People offer vegetarian sacrifices to me.
  • 無血食之養 - Without the nourishment of meat offerings.
  • 故無威福之靈 - Therefore, I have no power or blessing.
  • 今須復封我為王 - Now I must be re-enfeoffed as a king.
  • 當有威靈 - Then I will have power and blessings.
  • 魏公遂乞復其封 - Thus, Zhang Wei requested the restoration of his title.
  • 不知魏公是有此夢 - It is unknown whether Zhang Wei had this dream.
  • 是一時用兵 - Or it was a pretext for using military force.
  • 梓潼神極靈 - The Zitong deity is extremely powerful.
  • 此二神似乎割據兩川 - These two deities seem to dominate the two provinces.
  • 大抵鬼神用生物祭者 - Generally, deities that receive offerings of living things.
  • 皆是假此生气為靈 - Are made powerful by the vitality of these offerings.
  • 古人衅钟衅龟皆此意 - Ancient people sacrificed to bronze vessels and tortoise shells for this reason.
  • 漢卿云李通說 - Han Qing said that Li Tong said.
  • 有人射虎 - Someone shot a tiger.
  • 見虎后数人随之 - Saw several people following the tiger.
  • 乃是為虎伤死之人 - Were people who had been killed by the tiger.
  • 生气未散 - Their life force had not yet dissipated.
  • 故结成此形 - Therefore, they formed this shape.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 灌口二郎廟 - A famous temple in Sichuan province, dedicated to the Second Son of Li Bing, a legendary figure known for his contributions to flood control.
  • 李冰 - A historical figure who is credited with building the Dujiangyan irrigation system.
  • 徽宗 - Emperor Huizong of the Song Dynasty, known for his patronage of the arts and his interest in Daoism.
  • 張魏公 - Zhang Wei, a military leader during the Song Dynasty.
  • 梓潼神 - A deity worshipped in Zitong, Sichuan, known for his protective powers.
  • 兩川 - Refers to the regions of Sichuan and Shaanxi.
  • 衅钟衅龟 - Ancient rituals involving the sacrifice of bronze vessels and tortoise shells, believed to bring good fortune and ward off evil.
  • 漢卿 - Han Qing, a person mentioned in the text.
  • 李通 - Li Tong, a person mentioned in the text.

Proper Nouns

  • 灌口二郎廟 - The temple dedicated to the Second Son of Li Bing.
  • 李冰 - The historical figure who built the Dujiangyan irrigation system.
  • 徽宗 - Emperor Huizong of the Song Dynasty.
  • 張魏公 - Zhang Wei, a military leader during the Song Dynasty.
  • 梓潼神 - The deity worshipped in Zitong, Sichuan.
  • 漢卿 - Han Qing, a person mentioned in the text.
  • 李通 - Li Tong, a person mentioned in the text.

[816]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 亦有請得正身出現 - Cases where the spirit appears in its true form.
  • 其家小女子見 - A young girl from the family sees it.
  • 不知此是何物 - Does not know what this is.
  • 如衢州有一人事一神 - For example, in Quzhou, there is a person who serves a deity.
  • 只開所錄事目於紙，而封之祠前 - Only opens the list of matters written on paper and seals it in front of the shrine.
  • 少間开封，而纸中自有答语 - After a short time, when the paper is opened, there are answers already written on it.
  • 人秉天地之气生 - People are born by inheriting the qi of heaven and earth.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 小人 - Refers to 'petty people' or 'the unscrupulous,' a common term in classical Chinese for those of low moral character.

  • 守令 - Local magistrates or officials, a key administrative class in ancient China.

  • 修忏祝佛 - Performing repentance and making offerings to the Buddha, a common Buddhist practice.

  • 施转盛 - 'Donations are increasing,' indicating growing support or offerings.
  • 人情 - Human nature or human relations, a key concept in classical Chinese thought.

Proper Nouns

  • 佛 - A proper term for the Buddha.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 佛自西域而来 - Literal expression meaning 'Buddha came from the Western Regions,' referring to the introduction of Buddhism to China from Central Asia.
  • 其空虚清净之义 - Refers to the Buddhist concept of emptiness and purity, which can be interpreted as the idea of detachment from material desires and the pursuit of inner peace.
  • 驰鹜者息营求 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'those who rush about cease their pursuits,' suggesting that those who are overly active or driven will find peace by ceasing their relentless striving.
  • 忧愁者得排遣 - Means 'those who are worried can find relief,' indicating that Buddhism can provide solace to those who are troubled.
  • 因果报应之说 - Refers to the Buddhist doctrine of cause and effect, which teaches that actions have consequences.
  • 警戒下愚 - Means 'to warn the ignorant and foolish,' suggesting that Buddhism serves as a moral guide for those who lack wisdom.
  • 回心向善 - Means 'to turn one's heart toward goodness,' indicating a moral transformation.
  • 于世不为无补 - Means 'is not without benefit to the world,' suggesting that Buddhism has a positive impact on society.
  • 挟技之食客 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'a guest with skills,' suggesting that Buddhism is like a guest who brings valuable skills or knowledge to a host.
  • 食客不修其本技 - Means 'the guest does not refine his own skills,' suggesting that Buddhism does not focus on its own core teachings.
  • 变更主人之家政 - Means 'to change the master's household affairs,' suggesting that Buddhism tries to alter the fundamental practices of the host.
  • 使主人退而受教 - Means 'to make the master step back and receive instruction,' suggesting that Buddhism attempts to teach the host, which is inappropriate.
  • 佛者之过也 - Means 'it is the fault of Buddhism,' suggesting that Buddhism is at fault for its approach.
  • 各以末流而论 - Means 'considering the various branches,' suggesting that the discussion will focus on the different schools or interpretations of Buddhism.
  • 譬如种田 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'like farming,' suggesting that the discussion will use the analogy of agriculture to explain the relationship between Buddhism and Confucianism.
  • 儒犹耕耘者也 - Means 'Confucianism is like the farmer,' suggesting that Confucianism is the foundational and practical aspect of Chinese culture.
  • 佛家失其初旨 - Means 'Buddhism has lost its original purpose,' suggesting that Buddhism has deviated from its core teachings.
  • 不以善恶为罪福 - Means 'does not consider good and evil as causes of punishment and reward,' suggesting that Buddhism has shifted away from moral judgment.
  • 而以施舍不施舍为罪福 - Means 'but considers giving and not giving as causes of punishment and reward,' suggesting that Buddhism has focused on material charity instead of moral virtue.
  • 惑众蠹财 - Means 'deceiving the masses and wasting resources,' suggesting that Buddhism has led to the misallocation of resources and the spread of false beliefs.
  • 侵越疆畔 - Means 'trespassing boundaries,' suggesting that Buddhism has overstepped its proper role.
  • 攘窃禾稼 - Means 'stealing crops,' suggesting that Buddhism has taken resources that belong to others.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions, which were the areas west of China, including parts of Central Asia and India.
  • 空虚清净 - Refers to the Buddhist concepts of emptiness (śūnyatā) and purity, which are central to Buddhist philosophy.
  • 因果报应 - Refers to the Buddhist doctrine of karma, which teaches that actions have consequences.
  • 下愚 - Refers to the ignorant and foolish, a term often used in Confucian texts to describe those who lack moral and intellectual development.
  • 回心向善 - Refers to the moral transformation that is encouraged in both Buddhism and Confucianism, but the text suggests that Buddhism has not been effective in this regard.
  • 末流 - Refers to the various branches or schools of Buddhism, which have developed over time and may have diverged from the original teachings.
  • 种田 - Refers to farming, a common metaphor in Chinese literature to describe the cultivation of virtue and the foundation of society.

Proper Nouns

  • 佛 - Refers to Buddhism.
  • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions.
  • 中国 - Refers to China.

  • 虎竟避去 - 'The tiger ultimately fled,' suggesting that the tiger was impressed or frightened by the man’s bravery.
  • 自以為中黄，佽飞之流也 - A metaphorical expression. '中黄' and '佽飞' are names of legendary warriors or archers from ancient Chinese lore. The phrase means 'he considered himself to be in the same league as Zhong Huang and Ci Fei,' implying that he believed himself to be a hero or warrior of great renown.
  • 偶聞某寺後多鬼 - 'Accidentally heard that behind a certain temple there were many ghosts,' indicating a chance encounter with a rumor or legend.
  • 時嬲醉人 - 'Occasionally tease or torment drunkards,' where '嬲' is a classical term meaning to tease or bother, often in a playful or mischievous way.
  • 愤往驱逐 - 'Angered and went to drive them away,' indicating a strong emotional reaction to the reported ghostly activity.
  • 有好事数人随之往 - 'A few curious people followed him,' where '好事' refers to people who are eager to see or participate in unusual events.
  • 至则日薄暮 - 'When they arrived, it was nearly dusk,' indicating the time of day.
  • 乃纵饮至夜 - 'They then drank freely until night,' suggesting that they spent time drinking and relaxing before the main event.
  • 坐后垣上待其来 - 'Sat on the back wall waiting for them to come,' indicating a strategic or cautious position.
  • 二鼓后 - 'After the second drum,' a classical way of indicating time in the night, with the 'drum' marking the hour.
  • 隐隐闻啸声 - 'Faintly heard a howling sound,' describing the distant and eerie sound of the ghosts.
  • 乃大呼曰 - 'Then he shouted,' indicating a sudden and dramatic action.
  • 倏人影无数涌而至 - 'Suddenly, countless human figures rushed toward him,' indicating a sudden and overwhelming appearance of ghosts.
  • 皆吃吃笑曰 - 'All laughed mockingly,' where '吃吃笑' is a classical expression for laughter, often with a mocking or sneering tone.
  • 是尔耶，易也耳 - 'Is it you? It’s easy, after all,' a sarcastic or dismissive remark from the ghosts.
  • 耿怒跃下 - 'Gengang, angry, leaped down,' indicating a sudden and fierce reaction.
  • 则鸟兽散去 - 'Then they scattered like birds and beasts,' a metaphor for a sudden and chaotic dispersal.
  • 遥呼其名而詈之 - 'Cried out his name and scolded them,' indicating a verbal confrontation.
  • 东逐则在西，西逐则在东，此没彼出 - 'If he chased them east, they were west; if he chased them west, they were east; this one disappeared, that one appeared,' describing the elusive and shifting nature of the ghosts.
  • 倏忽千变 - 'Suddenly, a thousand transformations,' indicating rapid and unpredictable changes.
  • 耿旋转如风轮 - 'Gengang rotated like a wind wheel,' a metaphor for fast, circular movement.
  • 终不见一鬼 - 'Ultimately, he did not see a single ghost,' indicating his failure to catch any of them.
  • 疲极欲返 - 'Exhausted and wanting to return,' indicating his physical and mental fatigue.
  • 则嘲笑以激之 - 'Then they laughed at him and provoked him,' indicating the ghosts’ mockery and taunting.
  • 渐引渐远 - 'Gradually leading him further and further away,' indicating the ghosts luring him into a trap.
  • 突一奇鬼当路立 - 'Suddenly, a strange ghost stood in his path,' indicating a sudden and dangerous encounter.
  • 锯牙电目，张爪欲搏 - 'With saw-like teeth and electric eyes, stretching its claws to attack,' a vivid and menacing description of the ghost.
  • 急奋拳一击 - 'He quickly raised his fist and struck,' indicating a sudden and desperate action.
  • 忽噭然自仆 - 'Suddenly, it fell over,' indicating the ghost’s unexpected collapse.
  • 指已折掌已裂矣 - 'His finger was broken, his palm was shattered,' indicating the result of the strike.
  • 乃误击墓碑上也 - 'It turned out he had mistakenly struck a tombstone,' revealing the true cause of the ghost’s fall.
  • 群鬼合声曰：勇哉 - 'The group of ghosts shouted together: 'Brave indeed!' indicating their acknowledgment of his courage.
  • 瞥然俱沓 - 'Suddenly, they all vanished,' indicating the sudden disappearance of the ghosts.
  • 诸壁上观者闻耿呼痛，共持炬舁归 - 'The people watching from the walls heard Gengang crying in pain, and together they held torches and carried him back,' indicating the response of the onlookers.
  • 卧数日乃能起 - 'He lay for several days before he could rise,' indicating the severity of his injury.
  • 右手遂废 - 'His right hand became useless,' indicating permanent damage.
  • 从此猛气都尽，竟唾面自干焉 - 'From then on, his fierce spirit was completely gone; he even let spittle dry on his face,' a metaphor for losing all ambition or pride and becoming passive or defeated.
  • 夫能与虓虎敌，而不能不为鬼所困，虎斗力，鬼斗智也 - A concluding moral statement: 'One who can fight a roaring tiger yet cannot avoid being trapped by ghosts—tigers fight with strength, while ghosts fight with wit.'
  • 以有限之力，欲胜无穷之变幻，非天下之痴人乎？ - 'Using limited strength to overcome infinite transformations—was this not the act of a fool in the world?' a rhetorical question highlighting the futility of the endeavor.
  • 然一惩即戒，毅然自返，虽谓之大智慧人，亦可也 - 'Yet after one punishment, he was immediately warned, resolutely returned, and though he may be called a great wise person, it is also acceptable.' A statement of redemption and reflection.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 毛其人 - Likely a name or a title; could be a storyteller or a recorder of tales. The name is not a known historical figure, so it is best left as is.
  • 中黄，佽飞 - These are names of legendary warriors or archers from ancient Chinese lore. Zhong Huang and Ci Fei are figures from historical texts and myths, often associated with bravery and skill in combat.
  • 某寺 - A specific temple, though unnamed. In Classical Chinese, it is common to refer to places without specific names, so it should remain as 'a certain temple.'
  • 二鼓后 - Refers to the time of night in the traditional Chinese system of timekeeping, where the night was divided into five or six periods marked by the beating of drums.
  • 墓碑 - A tombstone, a common feature in ancient Chinese cemeteries and a symbol of death and the supernatural.
  • 唾面自干 - A metaphor from classical Chinese literature, meaning to endure humiliation without retaliation, often associated with the phrase 'spitting on one's face and letting it dry'—a symbol of complete defeat or resignation.
  • 夫能与虓虎敌，而不能不为鬼所困 - This is a moral reflection on the difference between brute force (tigers) and wit (ghosts), a common theme in classical Chinese literature and philosophy.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 本集皆不載 - Not included in the collection, indicating that the poems were omitted from the official anthology.
  • 诋诃前辈 - To criticize or rebuke one's predecessors, a term that carries a negative connotation, suggesting disapproval or harsh criticism.
  • 微涉讦直 - Slightly involving direct accusation or confrontation, indicating a tone of confrontation or directness that might be considered inappropriate.
  • 编集时自删之欤 - Was it deleted during the compilation? This phrase suggests that the omission was deliberate, possibly due to the content's controversial nature.
  • 赠庆大司马丹年 - Presented to Grand Marshal Danian, where 'Grand Marshal' is a high-ranking military title, and 'Danian' is likely a personal name or title.
  • 刘石庵参知颇疑其伪 - Liu Shianan, the Censor-in-Chief, suspected it was fake, indicating skepticism about the authenticity of the text.
  • 古人多有集外诗 - Ancient people often had poems outside their collections, suggesting that it was not uncommon for poets to have works that were not included in their published anthologies.
  • 当是孙公传庭所亲制 - It is likely made by Sun Chuanjing himself, indicating that the authorship is attributed to a specific person.
  • 二砚大小相近 - The two stones are similar in size, describing the physical characteristics of the stones.
  • 姚安公以皆前代名臣，合为一匣 - Grampa Yao, considering they were both famous ministers from previous dynasties, put them together in one box, showing the respect and value placed on these items.
  • 汝佶夭逝 - Rujie died young, indicating a premature death.
  • 二砚为婢媪所窃卖 - The two stones were stolen and sold by maidservants, suggesting a loss due to theft.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 二诗本集皆不载 - Indicates that the poems were not included in the official collection, possibly due to their controversial nature.
  • 诋诃前辈 - Criticizing or rebuking one's predecessors was a sensitive topic in classical Chinese culture, often seen as disrespectful.
  • 微涉讦直 - Direct confrontation or accusation was generally frowned upon in classical Chinese society, especially when directed at respected figures.
  • 编集时自删之欤 - The process of compiling literary works was often meticulous and selective, and omissions could be deliberate.
  • 赠庆大司马丹年 - Grand Marshal was a high-ranking military position, and the act of presenting a gift to such a figure was significant.
  • 刘石庵参知颇疑其伪 - The role of Censor-in-Chief was to oversee and critique government officials, and suspicion of forgery was a serious charge.
  • 古人多有集外诗 - Many ancient poets had unpublished works, which sometimes revealed more personal or controversial aspects of their lives.
  • 当是孙公传庭所亲制 - Sun Chuanjing was a historical figure known for his contributions to literature and governance.
  • 二砚大小相近 - The similarity in size of the stones suggests a deliberate pairing or matching.
  • 姚安公以皆前代名臣，合为一匣 - The act of combining items from different eras in a single container shows a respect for historical continuity and the value of these items.
  • 汝佶夭逝 - Premature death was a common theme in classical Chinese literature, often evoking sympathy and reflection.
  • 二砚为婢媪所窃卖 - Theft by servants was a common problem in classical Chinese households, often leading to the loss of valuable items.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 河豚惟天津至多 - Literal statement meaning 'river pufferfish are most abundant in Tianjin,' emphasizing local abundance.
  • 土人食之，如园蔬 - Means 'the locals eat it as if it were garden vegetables,' highlighting the commonality of the practice.
  • 然亦恒有死者，不必家家皆善烹治也 - Concessive statement meaning 'yet there are still deaths, not because every household is skilled in cooking it,' explaining potential dangers.
  • 姨丈惕园牛公言 - Formal introduction of a narrative, meaning 'Aunt's husband, Mr. Ti Yuan Niu, said.'
  • 有一人嗜河豚，卒中毒死 - Narrative meaning 'there was a man who loved river pufferfish, and he eventually died from poisoning,' narrating a specific incident.
  • 死后见梦于妻子曰 - Narrative continuation meaning 'after his death, he appeared in a dream to his wife and children saying.'
  • 祀我何以无河豚耶 - Means 'why is there no river pufferfish in my offerings?' expressing a desire for the fish in posthumous rituals.
  • 此真死而无悔也 - Judgmental statement meaning 'this is truly dying without regret,' evaluating the man's obsession.
  • 又姚安公言 - Another formal introduction of a narrative, meaning 'Mr. Yao An said.'
  • 里有人，粗温饱，后以博破家 - Narrative meaning 'in the village, there was a man who was roughly well-fed and warm, but later lost his family due to gambling,' narrating another specific incident.
  • 临殁语其子曰 - Narrative continuation meaning 'on his deathbed, he told his son.'
  • 必以博具置棺中 - Means 'you must place the gambling tools in the coffin,' giving a specific instruction.
  • 如无鬼，与白骨同为土耳 - Conditional statement meaning 'if there is no ghost, it will be just like a pile of white bones,' explaining one possible outcome.
  • 于事何害 - Means 'what harm is there to this?' questioning the practicality.
  • 如有鬼，荒榛蔓草之间，非此何以消遣耶 - Conditional statement meaning 'if there is a ghost, in the wilderness and overgrown grass, what else can they do for amusement?' explaining another possible outcome.
  • 比大殓 - Narrative continuation meaning 'when the coffin was closed.'
  • 佥曰 - Means 'everyone said,' indicating a collective response.
  • 死葬之以礼，乱命不可从也 - Statement of tradition meaning 'bury him according to ritual, we cannot follow this strange command,' rejecting the son's request.
  • 独不云事死如事生乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'don't you say that treating the dead is like treating the living?' questioning the reasoning.
  • 生不能几谏，殁乃违之乎 - Statement of duty meaning 'if I couldn't advise him when he was alive, should I go against his wishes now that he's dead?' expressing a moral dilemma.
  • 我不讲学，诸公勿干预人家事 - Statement of neutrality meaning 'I don't study Confucianism, so you gentlemen shouldn't interfere in family matters,' deferring to others.
  • 卒从其命 - Means 'they ultimately followed his command,' concluding the narrative.
  • 姚安公曰 - Formal conclusion meaning 'Mr. Yao An said.'
  • 非礼也，然亦孝思无已之心也 - Mixed judgment meaning 'it is not proper, but it also shows an unending heart of filial piety,' evaluating the act.
  • 吾恶夫事事遵古礼，而思亲之心，则漠然者也 - Final statement meaning 'I dislike those who follow all ancient rituals, but have a cold-hearted attitude towards their parents,' expressing a personal opinion.

  • This opening sequence must be read as a single descriptive statement about the abundance and commonality of river pufferfish in Tianjin.
  • The concessive clause must be read as explaining the potential dangers despite the common practice.
  • This narrative sequence must be read as a continuous story about a man who died from eating river pufferfish.
  • The narrative continuation must be read as the man's appearance in a dream to his family.
  • The judgmental statement must be read as evaluating the man's obsession.
  • This narrative sequence must be read as a continuous story about a man who lost his family due to gambling.
  • The narrative continuation must be read as the man's final instructions to his son.
  • The conditional statement must be read as explaining one possible outcome.
  • The questioning statement must be read as challenging the practicality.
  • The conditional statement must be read as explaining another possible outcome.
  • The narrative continuation must be read as the collective response to the son's request.
  • The rhetorical question must be read as challenging the reasoning.
  • The statement of duty must be read as expressing a moral dilemma.
  • The statement of neutrality must be read as deferring to others.
  • The narrative continuation must be read as concluding the story.
  • The formal conclusion must be read as evaluating the act.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 病瘵 - Refers to tuberculosis, a common disease in ancient times, often associated with a wasting illness.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '一奴子业针工，其父母鬻身时，未鬻此子，故独别居于外' introduces the background of the servant and his living situation.
  • The sentence '其妇年二十余，为狐所媚，岁余病瘵死' describes the woman's encounter with the fox and her subsequent death.
  • The sentence '初不肯自言，病甚，乃言狐初来时为女形，自言新来邻舍也' explains the woman's initial reluctance to speak about the encounter and her eventual disclosure.
  • The sentence '留与语，渐涉谑，继而渐相逼，遽前拥抱，遂昏昏如魇' describes the progression of the fox's interactions with the woman.
  • The sentence '每夜辄来，必换一形，忽男忽女，忽老忽少，忽丑忽好，忽僧忽道，忽鬼忽神，忽今衣冠忽古衣冠' lists the various forms the fox takes.
  • The sentence '岁余无一重复者' emphasizes the uniqueness of each transformation.
  • The sentence '至则四肢缓纵，口噤不能言，惟心目中了了而已' describes the physical and mental state of the woman during the encounters.
  • The sentence '狐亦不交一言，不知为一狐所化，抑众狐更番而来也' raises the question of whether one fox or multiple foxes were involved.
  • The sentence '尤怪者，小姑偶入其室，突遇狐出，一跃即逝' describes a particularly strange encounter involving the woman's younger sister-in-law.
  • The sentence '小姑所见是方巾道袍人，白须瞏瞏，妇所见则黯黑垢腻，一卖煤人耳' describes the different appearances of the fox to the two women.
  • The sentence '同时异状，更不可思议耳' emphasizes the extreme strangeness of the event.

  • 已早为经纪 - I have already arranged it; 已: already; 为: to do; 经纪: arrange.
  • 但至否未可知 - But whether it will happen is uncertain; 但: but; 至否: whether it will happen; 未可知: uncertain.
  • 故先不奉告 - So I didn't tell you beforehand; 故: so; 先: beforehand; 不奉告: didn't tell you.
  • 旬月后当有耗耳 - In a month or two, you will hear something; 旬月: a month or two; 后: later; 当有耗: you will hear something; 耳: particle indicating a statement.

  • 恒耿耿不忘 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'I have always been deeply grateful.'
  • 今君别鹤离鸾，自合为君料理 - Narrative phrase meaning 'Now that you are separated from your beloved, it is fitting for me to take care of you.'

  • 因摄招花妖先至君家中半年 - Narrative phrase meaning 'So I summoned the flower demon to your home half a year ago.'

  • 并刺知家中旧事 - Narrative phrase meaning 'and learned about the old affairs of your household.'
  • 便君有证不疑 - Narrative phrase meaning 'so that you would have no doubt.'

  • 渠皆幻相，君勿复思 - Narrative phrase meaning 'They are all illusions, do not think about them again.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • Read '尔辈念主人恩耶？倘不死，会有日相报耳' as a single conditional statement.
  • Read '皆不答，惟趣为生治装' as a single concise statement.
  • Read '濒行，翁治酒作饯，并呼三女出曰' as a single descriptive phrase.
  • Read '今日事须明言矣' as a single formal declaration.
  • Read '因拱手对生曰' as a single narrative phrase.
  • Read '老夫地仙也，过去生中，与君为同官，殁后君百计营求，归吾妻子，恒耿耿不忘' as a single long narrative phrase.
  • Read '今君别鹤离鸾，自合为君料理' as a single narrative phrase.
  • Read '但山川绵邈，二孱弱女子，何以能来' as a single rhetorical question.
  • Read '因摄招花妖先至君家中半年' as a single narrative phrase.
  • Read '窥尊室容貌语言，摹拟具似，并刺知家中旧事' as a single narrative phrase.
  • Read '便君有证不疑' as a single narrative phrase.
  • Read '渠本三姊妹，故多增一婢耳' as a single narrative phrase.
  • Read '渠皆幻相，君勿复思' as a single narrative phrase.
  • Read '到家相对旧人，仍与此间无异矣' as a single narrative phrase.

  • 三女握手作別，洒泪沾衣 - Description of the three daughters holding hands to say goodbye, with tears wetting their clothes.
  • 俯仰间已俱不见 - Phrase indicating that in a moment, the three daughters disappeared.
  • 登舟时遥见立岸上，招之不至 - Description of the narrator seeing the three daughters standing on the shore when boarding the boat, but they did not come closer.
  • 归后，妻子具言家日落，赖君岁岁寄金来，得活至今，盖亦此叟所为也 - After returning, the narrator's wife and children explained that the family was struggling, but thanks to the annual gold sent by the narrator, they managed to survive, which was likely due to the old man's help.
  • 使世间离别人，皆逢此叟，则无复牛衣银河之恨矣 - Hypothetical statement suggesting that if all parting people could meet this old man, there would be no more sorrow like the separation of the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl.
  • 信然，然粤东有地仙，他处亦必有地仙，董仙有此术，他仙亦必有此术，所以无人再逢者，当由过去生中，原未受恩，胡不肯竭尽心力，缩地补天耳 - Concluding statement affirming the old man's existence and suggesting that similar beings exist elsewhere, but people do not meet them because they have not received their help in past lives, and thus do not exert themselves to find them.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 客作田不满 - The name '不满' (Bùmǎn) is a play on words. '不满' can mean 'not satisfied' or 'impatient,' but it is also a homophone for '饕餮' (Tāotiè), a mythical creature known for its insatiable appetite. This suggests that the character is named after a legendary beast, which adds a layer of meaning to the story.
  • 取不自满假之义 - This phrase means 'taking the meaning of not being self-satisfied.' It suggests that the name '不满' was originally intended to convey a moral lesson about humility.
  • 既乃知以饕餮得此名，取田填同音也 - This explains that the name '不满' is actually derived from the homophone '饕餮' and '田填' (Tiántián), which both sound similar in Chinese.
  • 夜行失道，误经墟墓间 - This phrase means 'walking at night and losing one's way, mistakenly passing through a burial ground.' It sets the scene for the encounter with the skull.
  • 蹋一骷髅，骷髅作声曰 - This describes the protagonist stepping on a skull, which then speaks. The use of '作声' (zàoshēng) means 'to make a sound' or 'to speak.'
  • 毋败我面，且祸尔 - This is a direct quote from the skull, meaning 'do not damage my face, and I will harm you.' It shows the skull's fear of being disfigured.
  • 不满戆且悍，叱曰 - This describes the protagonist as 'stupid and fierce' and shows his response with a shout. '戆' (gàng) means 'stupid' or 'foolish,' and '悍' (hàn) means 'fierce' or 'bold.'
  • 谁遣尔当路 - This means 'who sent you to block the path?' It is a rhetorical question expressing frustration.
  • 人移我于此，非我当路也 - This is the skull's response, meaning 'people moved me here, not that I wanted to block the path.' It explains that the skull is not intentionally obstructing the path.
  • 尔何不祸移尔者 - This means 'why don't you harm those who moved you?' It is another rhetorical question expressing frustration.
  • 彼运方盛，无如何也 - This means 'they are currently in a period of prosperity, so there's nothing to be done.' It suggests that the people who moved the skull are powerful and cannot be harmed.
  • 不满笑且怒曰 - This describes the protagonist's reaction, meaning 'he laughed and became angry.' It shows a mix of amusement and frustration.
  • 岂我衰耶？畏盛而凌衰，是何理耶？ - This is a rhetorical question, meaning 'am I in decline? To fear the strong and bully the weak, what kind of logic is that?' It expresses the protagonist's indignation.
  • 骷髅作泣曰 - This describes the skull's response, meaning 'the skull wept and said.' It shows the skull's emotional reaction.
  • 君气亦盛，故我不敢祟，徒以虚词恫喝也 - This means 'your qi (vital energy) is also strong, so I dare not harm you, I just used empty words to scare you.' It explains the skull's fear of the protagonist.
  • 畏盛凌衰，人情皆尔，君乃责鬼乎？ - This means 'to fear the strong and bully the weak, this is human nature, why do you blame the ghost?' It suggests that the protagonist's behavior is not unique to ghosts.
  • 哀而拨入土窟中，君之惠也 - This means 'moved it into a hole in the ground, which is your kindness.' It shows the protagonist's final action and his sense of compassion.
  • 不满冲之竟过，惟闻背后呜呜声，卒无他异 - This means 'he rushed past it, only hearing a wailing sound behind him, and nothing else unusual happened.' It describes the protagonist's reaction and the aftermath.
  • 余谓不满无仁心，然遇卤莽之人，而以大言激其怒，鬼亦有过焉 - This means 'I think Bùmǎn lacks compassion, but when he encountered a rude person and used grand words to provoke him, the ghost also had some fault.' It provides the narrator's reflection on the incident.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 饕餮 - A mythical creature from Chinese mythology known for its insatiable appetite. It is often depicted as a symbol of greed and gluttony.
  • 墟墓间 - A burial ground, which is a common setting in Chinese literature for supernatural encounters.
  • 骷髅 - A skull, which is often associated with death and the supernatural in Chinese folklore.
  • 运方盛 - A period of prosperity or good fortune, which is a common theme in Chinese literature and philosophy.
  • 畏盛凌衰 - To fear the strong and bully the weak, which is a critique of social injustice and power dynamics.
  • 人情皆尔 - Human nature is the same, suggesting that certain behaviors are universal.
  • 卤莽之人 - A rude or impulsive person, which is a common character type in Chinese literature.
  • 大言 - Grand or exaggerated words, often used to provoke or intimidate.
  • 鬼亦有过焉 - Even the ghost had some fault, suggesting that supernatural beings are not immune to criticism.

Proper Nouns

  • 墟墓间 - A burial ground, which is a common setting in Chinese literature for supernatural encounters.
  • 骷髅 - A skull, which is often associated with death and the supernatural in Chinese folklore.

  • 畏盛凌衰 - To fear the strong and bully the weak.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 心氣不寧 - Classical medical term meaning 'restlessness of the heart qi,' referring to a state of mental or emotional unease.
  • 稍作勞則如簌簌動 - When doing slight labor, it feels as if it is shivering or trembling. Metaphorical description of physical or emotional agitation.
  • 服枣仁远志之属 - Taking jujube seeds and Polygala (far志) and similar herbs. Refers to traditional herbal remedies used in TCM for calming the mind.
  • 时作时止，不甚验也 - Sometimes it occurs, sometimes it stops, but it is not very effective. Reflects the intermittent nature of the condition and the limited efficacy of the treatment.
  • 扶乩 - Fumigation divination or spirit-writing. A Taoist or folk practice in which a medium uses a rod or stick to write messages from a spirit.
  • 纯阳真人 - True Person of Pure Yang, a title for a Taoist deity or spiritual figure, likely referring to Lü Dongbin, one of the Eight Immortals.
  • 拜乞方 - To bow and request a formula (prescription). A respectful way of asking for medical advice from a spiritual figure.
  • 此证现于心，而其原出于脾 - This condition manifests in the heart, but its origin is in the spleen. Reflects classical Chinese medical theory, where the spleen is associated with the 'mother' organ of the heart.
  • 脾虚则子食母气故也 - Because the spleen is weak, the child (heart) feeds on the mother’s (spleen) qi. A metaphorical expression from traditional Chinese medicine, where the spleen is considered the 'mother' of the heart.
  • 炒白术常服之 - Take fried Atractylodes (Bai Zhu) regularly. This is a specific herbal remedy.
  • 试之果验 - Trying it proved effective. A common expression used to affirm the success of a treatment.
  • 场屋文字 - Exam hall writings, referring to the essays or compositions written by candidates during imperial examinations.
  • 笔酣墨饱 - The brush is full, the ink is rich. A metaphor for confident, fluent, and well-executed writing.
  • 书味盎然 - The writing is rich and full of flavor. Suggests that the writing is expressive and well-crafted.
  • 中式矣 - To pass the examination (to be admitted). A common term used in the context of the imperial exams.
  • 何必预问乎 - Why ask in advance? A rhetorical question implying that the outcome is already determined.
  • 登进士 - To be admitted as a jinshi (advanced degree holder).
  • 本房同考官 - The examiner in charge of the same section or chamber. Refers to the official who reviewed the candidate's exam.
  • 出阅卷簿视之 - To look at the exam records. Refers to the process of reviewing exam papers.
  • 所注批词即此八字也 - The comments written on it were exactly these eight characters. Refers to the specific phrase used by the examiner.
  • 科名前定，并批词亦前定乎 - If the degree is predestined, are the comments also predestined? A philosophical or mystical question about fate and predestination.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乾隆丙辰 - The year 1776 (the 33rd year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign), following the Chinese sexagenary cycle.

  • 批词 - The written comments or annotations made by the examiner on the candidate's essay.

  • 宜其以人始，以人终也 - A phrase meaning 'It is appropriate that it begins with humans and ends with humans,' suggesting the fox's journey or transformation begins and ends among humans.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 六道轮回 - A Buddhist concept, central to Chinese religious thought, which describes the cycle of rebirth across six realms of existence. This term is used to suggest that human behavior (especially negative or immoral behavior) can lead to rebirth in a lower realm, such as that of a fox.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 吴惠叔携一小幅挂轴，纸色似百年外物，云得之长椿寺市上 - This sentence introduces the artwork and its origin, but the phrase '云得之长椿寺市上' is somewhat ambiguous. It could mean 'it is said that he obtained it from the market at Changchun Temple' or 'he said he obtained it from the market at Changchun Temple.'
  • 笔墨草略，半以淡墨扫烟霭，半作水纹，中惟一小舟，一女子坐篷下，一女子摇橹而已 - This sentence describes the painting in detail, but the phrase '中惟一小舟，一女子坐篷下，一女子摇橹而已' is somewhat elliptical. It could be read as 'there is only a small boat in the middle, with one woman sitting under the awning and another woman rowing the boat.'
  • 右角浓墨，写一诗曰：沙鸥同住水云乡，不记荷花几度香，颇怪麻姑太多事，犹知人世有沧桑 - This sentence describes the poem inscribed on the artwork, but the phrase '颇怪麻姑太多事' is somewhat ambiguous. It could mean 'oddly enough, Mazu has done too many things' or 'it is odd that Mazu has been involved in so many things.'
  • 款曰：画中人自画并题。无年月，无印记 - This sentence describes the inscription on the artwork, but the phrase '无年月，无印记' is somewhat elliptical. It could be read as 'there is no date, no seal.'
  • 或以为仙笔，然女仙手迹，人何自得之，或以为游女，又不应作此世外语，疑是明末女冠，避兵于渔庄蟹舍，自作此图 - This sentence presents different theories about the origin of the artwork, but the phrase '或以为仙笔' is somewhat ambiguous. It could mean 'some believe it is a divine brush' or 'some think it is a divine brush.'
  • 无旧人跋语，亦难确信 - This sentence expresses doubt about the authenticity of the artwork, but the phrase '亦难确信' is somewhat elliptical. It could be read as 'it is also difficult to confirm.'
  • 惠叔索题，余无从著笔，置数日还之，惠叔殁于蜀中，此画不知今在否也 - This sentence describes the translator's reaction to the request to inscribe the artwork and the fate of the artwork, but the phrase '余无从著笔' is somewhat ambiguous. It could mean 'I cannot find a way to write about it' or 'I cannot find a way to inscribe it.'

  • 事与人异 - '事' means 'matters,' '与' means 'and,' and '人异' means 'are different from humans.'
  • 倏忽之往来 - '倏忽' means 'suddenly' or 'swiftly,' and '往来' means 'coming and going.'
  • 逞厥凶锋 - '逞' means 'to display' or 'to show off,' '厥' is a pronoun meaning 'his' or 'its,' and '凶锋' means 'evil sharpness' or 'evil force.'
  • 几戕其命 - '几' means 'almost,' '戕' means 'to kill,' and '其命' means 'his life.'
  • 狐可谓妒且悍矣 - '狐' means 'fox,' '可谓' means 'can be said,' '妒' means 'jealous,' and '悍' means 'fierce.'
  • 然本无夙约 - '然' means 'but,' '本' means 'originally,' '无' means 'no,' and '夙约' means 'previous agreement.'
  • 则曲在狐 - '则' means 'then,' '曲' means 'fault,' and '在狐' means 'lies with the fox.'
  • 既不慎于始而与约 - '既' means 'already,' '不慎' means 'careless,' '于始' means 'at the beginning,' and '与约' means 'to make an agreement.'
  • 又不善其终而背之 - '又' means 'also,' '不善' means 'not good,' '其终' means 'the end,' and '背之' means 'to break it.'
  • 则激而为祟 - '则' means 'then,' '激' means 'to provoke,' and '为祟' means 'to cause mischief.'
  • 亦自有词 - '亦' means 'also,' '自有' means 'has its own,' and '词' means 'reason' or 'excuse.'
  • 是固未可罪狐也 - '是' means 'this,' '固' means 'indeed,' '未可' means 'cannot,' '罪' means 'blame,' and '狐也' means 'the fox.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 后族 - Refers to the family of the empress or consort, often influential in court politics.
  • 狐女 - Refers to a fox spirit in the form of a woman, a common motif in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 神祠 - Refers to a shrine or temple dedicated to a deity or spirit.
  • 北方之俗 - Refers to customs and traditions specific to the northern regions of China.
  • 土墼 - Refers to clay bricks, commonly used in construction in ancient China.
  • 神祠无庙祝者 - Refers to shrines without a priest or caretaker, often left unattended.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '后族众强为纳妇，甲闻其女甚姣丽，遂顿负旧盟' introduces the main characters and the initial situation, where the man (甲) breaks his promise due to the woman's beauty.
  • The complex sentence '迨洞房停烛之时，突声若风霆，震撼檐宇，一手破窗而入，其大如箕，攫某甲以去' describes the sudden and violent appearance of the fox spirit, emphasizing its size and strength.
  • The sequence '次日，四出觅访，杳然无迹，七八日后，有数小儿言某神祠中有声如牛喘' describes the search for the man and the discovery of the fox spirit in a shrine.
  • The clause '北方之俗，凡神祠无庙祝者，虑流丐栖息，多以土墼癬其户，而留一穴置香炉' explains the custom of leaving a small hole in the door of unattended shrines to prevent wandering beggars from staying.
  • The sequence '自穴窥之，似有一人裸体卧，不辨为谁，启户视之，则某甲在焉' describes the discovery of the man in the shrine, emphasizing his confused state.
  • The phrase '已昏昏不知人矣' describes the man's condition, indicating he is unconscious or confused.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 易姤卦之初六 - Refers to the first line of the Guai hexagram in the *I Ching* (Book of Changes). '初六' means the first line of the hexagram, which is yin in nature.
  • 一陰始生 - Literally means 'the first yin begins to arise,' indicating the emergence of yin energy.
  • 系於金柅 - '系於' means 'tied to' or 'attached to,' and '金柅' refers to a metal stopper or brake. This is a metaphor for restraint.
  • 柅以止車 - '止車' means 'to stop a carriage,' reinforcing the metaphor of restraint.
  • 示當止也 - Means 'to show that one should stop,' emphasizing the need for caution.
  • 不止則履霜之初，即堅冰之漸 - '不止' means 'if not stopped,' and '履霜之初' refers to the beginning of walking on frost, which is a metaphor for the early signs of a problem. '即堅冰之漸' means 'it will gradually become solid ice,' indicating a worsening situation.
  • 浸假而剝卦六五至矣 - '浸假' means 'gradually' or 'over time,' and '剝卦六五' refers to the fifth line of the Po hexagram, which is yang in nature. This suggests a significant transformation.
  • 今日之事，是之謂乎 - '是之謂乎' is a rhetorical question meaning 'is this what it means?'
  • 然苟無其隙，雖小人不能伺 - '然' means 'however,' '苟無其隙' means 'if there is no opening,' and '雖小人不能伺' means 'even a small person cannot take advantage.'
  • 苟無所好，雖小人不能投 - '苟無所好' means 'if there is nothing desirable,' and '雖小人不能投' means 'even a small person cannot throw themselves in.'
  • 千金之堤，潰於蟻漏 - '千金之堤' refers to a dike worth a thousand gold pieces, and '潰於蟻漏' means 'is destroyed by ant holes,' emphasizing how small problems can lead to big disasters.
  • 有罅故也 - '有罅' means 'there is a crack,' and '故也' means 'therefore.'
  • 公先誤涉旁門，欲講容成之術 - '誤涉旁門' means 'mistakenly entered a side door,' and '容成之術' refers to a method attributed to Rong Cheng, a legendary figure associated with longevity and alchemy.
  • 既而耽玩艷冶，失其初心 - '耽玩' means 'to indulge in,' '艷冶' refers to beautiful and alluring things, and '失其初心' means 'lost his original intention.'
  • 嗜欲日深，故妖物乘之而麇集 - '嗜欲日深' means 'desires become deeper,' and '故妖物乘之而麇集' means 'therefore, evil spirits take advantage and gather.'
  • 衅因自起，於彼何尤 - '衅因自起' means 'the cause arises from oneself,' and '於彼何尤' means 'what fault does it have with others?'
  • 此始此終，固亦其理 - '此始此終' means 'from beginning to end,' and '固亦其理' means 'it is indeed its nature.'
  • 驅之而不谴，蓋以是耳 - '驅之而不谴' means 'to drive them away without blame,' and '蓋以是耳' means 'it is because of this.'
  • 吾來稍晚，於公事已無益 - '吾來稍晚' means 'I came a bit late,' and '於公事已無益' means 'it is of no benefit to public affairs.'
  • 然從此攝心清靜，猶不失作九十翁 - '然從此攝心清靜' means 'however, from now on, if one calms the mind and remains quiet,' and '猶不失作九十翁' means 'one can still become a ninety-year-old man.'
  • 再三珍重 - Means 'take care repeatedly' or 'take good care.'
  • 瞥然而去 - Means 'left suddenly' or 'departed swiftly.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 易姤卦 - Refers to the Guai hexagram in the *I Ching* (Book of Changes), which is associated with unexpected encounters or unions.
  • 金柅 - A metal stopper or brake, symbolizing restraint and control.
  • 容成之術 - A method attributed to Rong Cheng, a legendary figure associated with longevity and alchemy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以為 - A conjunction used to introduce a mistaken belief or assumption; meaning 'to think' or 'to consider.'

  • 莫或過問也 - A formal expression meaning 'nobody dared to question it' or 'nobody interfered with it.'
  • 意甚匆遽 - Means 'his mind was very hurried' or 'he was in a great rush.'
  • 急買舟欲赴天目山 - 'Urgently bought a boat to go to Tianmu Mountain,' indicating a sense of urgency.
  • 求高行僧作道場 - 'Sought out a high-minded monk to conduct a Buddhist ritual or ceremony (dào chǎng).'
  • 僧以其疏語掩抑支離 - The monk found the person’s written request (疏, *shū*, a formal letter or petition) to be obscure, incoherent, or evasive.
  • 不知何事 - 'Did not know what the matter was.'
  • 又有本是佛传 - 'Also, the text was based on a Buddhist scripture (佛传, *fó chuán*), suggesting it was a religious text.'
  • 当求佛佑，仰藉慈云之庇，庶宽雷部之刑语 - 'One should seek the Buddha's protection, relying on the mercy of the compassionate cloud (慈云), so that the punishment of the Thunder Department (雷部) might be lenient.' This is a religious or mythological expression involving Buddhist and Daoist elements.
  • 疑有别故 - 'Suspected there was a different reason' or 'suspected there was an unusual cause.'
  • 还其衬施，谢遣之 - 'Returned the offering (衬施, *chèn shī*, a donation or alms) and sent him away in gratitude.'
  • 至中途，果殒于雷 - 'When he reached the middle of the journey, he was indeed struck down by lightning.' This is a supernatural or mythological event.
  • 后从者微泄其事 - 'Later, his attendants discreetly revealed the matter.'
  • 此人从一红衣番僧受异术 - 'This person had received strange or magical techniques from a red-robed foreign monk (番僧, *fān sēng*, a term for foreign or non-Chinese monks).'
  • 能持咒摄取新敛女子尸 - 'Able to recite mantras to capture the body of a newly deceased woman.'
  • 又摄取妖狐淫鬼，附其尸以生 - 'Also able to capture妖狐 (妖狐, *yāo hú*, a fox spirit or demon) and淫鬼 (淫鬼, *yín guǐ*, lustful ghosts or demons), and attach them to the body to bring it to life.'
  • 即以自侍 - 'And use it to serve himself' or 'and use it as a companion.'
  • 再有新者，即以旧者转售人，获利无算 - 'When a new one comes, he sells the old one to others, making huge profits.' This indicates a cycle of selling captured bodies.
  • 因梦神责以恶贯将满，当伏天诛 - 'Because he dreamed of a deity accusing him of having accumulated enough evil deeds to warrant heavenly punishment.'
  • 故忏悔以求免，竟不能也 - 'Therefore, he repented to seek forgiveness, but in the end, it was not enough.'
  • 疑此客之妇，即为此人所摄矣 - 'It is suspected that the wife of this traveler was captured by this person.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天目山 - A mountain in Zhejiang Province, historically associated with Daoist and Buddhist monasteries and considered a place of spiritual power.

  • 慈云 - A metaphorical or poetic reference to the compassion or mercy of the Buddha or a deity.
  • 红衣番僧 - A term for a foreign or non-Chinese Buddhist monk, possibly from Tibet or Central Asia, often associated with esoteric or mystical practices.
  • 妖狐 - A fox spirit or shapeshifting demon in Chinese folklore, often associated with seduction and supernatural abilities.
  • 淫鬼 - A lustful ghost or demon, often linked to sexual transgressions or corruption.
  • 红教 - Refers to the Nyingma school of Tibetan Buddhism, one of the oldest schools, sometimes associated with more esoteric or mystical practices.
  • 黄教 - Refers to the Gelug school of Tibetan Buddhism, the most recent and dominant school, often associated with more orthodox and institutionalized practices.
  • 理藩院 - A Qing dynasty government body responsible for managing relations with ethnic minorities and foreign nations.
  • 留公 - A title used to refer to a high-ranking official, likely the Minister of the Board of Colonial Affairs.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 水清讵免双螯黑，秋老难逃一背红 - Poetic couplet using metaphor to criticize greed; '水清' (clear water) and '秋老' (autumn) set the scene, while '双螯黑' (black claws) and '一背红' (red back) describe the physical characteristics of crabs, symbolizing the inevitability of greed leading to downfall.
  • 借寓朱勔之贪婪必败也 - '借寓' means 'to use as a metaphor,' and '朱勔' is a historical figure known for his greed; the phrase indicates that the poem is a metaphorical criticism of his greed and its inevitable consequences.
  • 他物供庖厨，一死焉而已 - '他物' refers to other creatures, '供庖厨' means 'to be offered to the kitchen,' and '一死焉而已' means 'nothing more than a single death'; this phrase suggests that other animals, when caught, face a quick and simple death.
  • 惟蟹则生投釜甑，徐受蒸煮 - '惟' means 'only,' '生投' means 'thrown alive,' '釜甑' refers to cooking pots, and '徐受蒸煮' means 'slowly subjected to steaming and boiling'; this describes the cruel fate of crabs, who are thrown alive into pots and slowly cooked.
  • 由初沸至熟，至速亦逾数刻 - '由初沸至熟' means 'from the initial boiling to being cooked,' and '至速亦逾数刻' means 'even the fastest process takes several moments'; this emphasizes the prolonged suffering of the crabs.
  • 其楚毒有求死不得者 - '其楚毒' means 'their suffering,' and '有求死不得者' means 'those who cannot even die'; this phrase highlights the intense pain and helplessness of the crabs.
  • 意非夙业深重，不堕是中 - '意非' means 'it is not,' '夙业深重' means 'deep karmic debt,' and '不堕是中' means 'not falling into this state'; this suggests that the crabs' suffering is due to their deep karmic debt.

  • 外祖安公 - Refers to the author's maternal grandfather, '安公' being his courtesy name.
  • 前母安太夫人父也 - Indicates that the maternal grandfather is the father of the author's deceased mother, '安太夫人.'
  • 殁时家尚盛 - Means 'when he died, the family was still prosperous.'
  • 诸舅多以金宝殉 - Refers to the practice of burying valuables with the deceased, here specifically mentioning the maternal uncles.
  • 或陈璠玙之戒，不省 - '璠玙' are precious stones, and '戒' means 'advice' or 'warning.' The phrase suggests that someone advised against burying valuables, but the family did not heed the advice.
  • 筑室墓垣外 - Means 'build a house outside the tomb wall.'
  • 以数壮夫逻守 - Refers to hiring several strong men to guard the tomb.
  • 柝声铃声，彻夜相答 - Describes the sound of wooden clappers and bells echoing through the night.
  • 是树帜招盗也 - Suggests that the guards' vigilance might be interpreted as a signal to thieves.
  • 既而果被发 - Means 'soon after, the tomb was indeed robbed.'
  • 乘守者昼寝 - Refers to the guards sleeping during the day.
  • 衣青蓑，矴垣伏草间 - Describes the robbers dressing in green cloaks and hiding in the grass near the tomb wall.
  • 以椎凿破棺 - Means 'using a hammer to break open the coffin.'
  • 柝二击则亦二椎，柝三击则亦三椎 - Describes a pattern where the guards' clapping matches the robbers' hammer blows, leading to a false sense of security.
  • 伏至天欲晓，铃柝皆息，乃矴垣遁 - Means 'they hid until dawn, when all the sounds stopped, and then they fled through the wall.'
  • 一含珠巨如龙眼核，亦裂颏取去 - Describes a large pearl as big as a lychee pit that was also taken by the robbers.
  • 告官大索 - Means 'reporting to the authorities for a large-scale search.'
  • 吾夙生负此三人财，今取偿捕亦不获 - Suggests that the deceased believed he owed money to three people in a past life and that the robbers were taking revenge.
  • 惟我未尝屠割彼，而横见酷虐，刃眘断我颐 - Means 'but I never harmed them, and yet I suffered cruelly, with my jaw cut off.'
  • 得直于冥司矣 - Means 'I have received justice in the underworld.'
  • 灼知姓名 - Refers to identifying the robbers' names through some means, possibly through interrogation or other methods.
  • 千金购捕不能得 - Means 'even a reward of a thousand gold pieces could not catch them.'
  • 梦语不诬矣 - Suggests that the dream was not false.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 外祖安公 - The author's maternal grandfather, a figure of respect and authority.
  • 前母安太夫人 - The author's deceased mother, whose family is being discussed.
  • 金宝 - Precious items often buried with the dead in ancient Chinese funerary practices.
  • 璠玙 - Precious stones, often used in jewelry and as symbols of wealth.
  • 筑室墓垣外 - Building a house outside the tomb wall was a common practice to honor the deceased and provide a place for rituals.
  • 逻守 - Guarding the tomb, often done by family members or hired guards.
  • 柝声铃声 - The sounds of wooden clappers and bells, used to signal and maintain vigilance.
  • 树帜招盗 - A belief that visible signs of wealth or vigilance might attract thieves.
  • 被发 - Tomb robbery, a common problem in ancient China.
  • 昼寝 - Guards falling asleep during the day, a critical lapse in vigilance.
  • 衣青蓑 - Dressing in green cloaks, a way to blend in with the environment.
  • 矴垣伏草间 - Hiding in the grass near the tomb wall, a strategic position for the robbers.
  • 椎凿破棺 - Using tools to break open the coffin, a violent act.
  • 伏至天欲晓 - Hiding until dawn, a time when the guards might be less vigilant.
  • 铃柝皆息 - All sounds stopping, indicating that the guards were no longer alert.
  • 矴垣遁 - Fleeing through the wall, a clever escape method.
  • 含珠 - A pearl placed in the mouth of the deceased, a common practice in ancient China.
  • 裂颏取去 - Cutting open the jaw to remove the pearl, a brutal act.
  • 告官大索 - Reporting the crime to the authorities for a large-scale search.
  • 夙生负此三人财 - Belief in past-life debts and karma, a concept in Chinese Buddhism and Taoism.
  • 得直于冥司矣 - Receiving justice in the underworld, a belief in the afterlife.
  • 灼知姓名 - Identifying the robbers' names, possibly through interrogation or other means.
  • 千金购捕不能得 - A large reward offered but not sufficient to catch the robbers.
  • 梦语不诬矣 - The dream was not false, suggesting that the deceased's words were true.

  • 外祖安公 - The author's maternal grandfather.
  • 前母安太夫人 - The author's deceased mother.
  • 诸舅 - The author's maternal uncles.
  • 璠玙 - Precious stones.
  • 安太夫人 - The author's deceased mother.

  • 驅骡纲行山间 - 驅 means 'to drive,' 驴纲 refers to a caravan of mules, and 行山间 means 'traveling through the mountains.' This phrase describes a group of merchants driving a mule caravan through the mountains.

  • 谪仙 - 谪仙 means 'banished immortal,' referring to someone who was an immortal but has been demoted to a human form.

  • 残骸狼藉 - 残骸 means 'remains,' and 狼藉 means 'scattered.' This phrase describes scattered remains, likely from an animal attack.

  • 无故而致非常之福 - 无故 means 'without cause,' 而致 means 'to bring,' 非常之福 means 'extraordinary good fortune.' This phrase describes receiving unexpected good fortune.
  • 贪冒者所喜 - 贪冒 means 'greedy and rash,' and 所喜 means 'what they like.' This phrase describes what greedy and rash people desire.
  • 明哲者所惧 - 明哲 means 'wise and discerning,' and 所惧 means 'what they fear.' This phrase describes what wise and discerning people fear.
  • 无故而作非分之想 - 无故 means 'without cause,' 而作 means 'to make,' 非分之想 means 'unreasonable desires.' This phrase describes having unreasonable desires without cause.
  • 侥悻者其偶 - 侥悻 means 'lucky and fortunate,' and 其偶 means 'their chance.' This phrase describes what lucky and fortunate people might experience.
  • 颠越者其常 - 颠越 means 'upset and out of control,' and 其常 means 'their usual state.' This phrase describes what upset and out of control people usually experience.
  • 谓此人之鲁钝，正此人之聪明可矣 - 谓 means 'to say,' 此人 means 'this person,' 鲁钝 means 'stupid and dull,' 正 means 'just,' 聪明 means 'intelligent,' 可矣 means 'can be said.' This phrase suggests that calling this person stupid and dull is actually a sign of intelligence.

  • 阻水而返 - 阻 means to be blocked by, 水 means water, and 而返 means and return. Indicates that the protagonist was blocked by water and had to return.
  • 痴立良久 - 痴立 means to stand dazedly or motionlessly, and 良久 means for a long time. Describes the protagonist standing motionless for a long time.
  • 折而东北入豆田 - 折而 means turned and, 东北 means northeast, and 入豆田 means entered the bean field. Describes the protagonist turning northeast and entering a bean field.
  • 诘屈行，颠踬者再 - 诘屈行 means to walk with difficulty or in a winding path, and 颠踬者再 means stumbled twice. Describes the protagonist's difficult and winding journey, during which they stumbled twice.
  • 几嫂深夜往何处 - 几嫂 means my wife, 深夜 means deep night, and 往何处 means where are you going. The protagonist's question to his wife.
  • 迤北更无路，且陷淖中矣 - 迤北 means northward, 更无路 means no more road, and 且陷淖中矣 means and you are sinking into the mud. The protagonist's warning to his wife.
  • 我不能出，几郎可领我还 - 我不能出 means I cannot get out, 几郎 means my husband, and 可领我还 means please guide me back. The wife's response to her husband.
  • 知其迷路，乃遥呼曰 - 知其迷路 means knew that she was lost, 乃 means then, and 遥呼曰 means shouted from afar. Describes the protagonist's realization and his shout to his wife.
  • 急赴之，已无睹矣 - 急赴之 means rushed to her, and 已无睹矣 means but saw nothing. Describes the protagonist's futile attempt to find his wife.
  • 知为遇鬼，心惊骨栗，狂奔归家 - 知为遇鬼 means knew that he had met a ghost, 心惊骨栗 means his heart was terrified and his bones shivered, and 狂奔归家 means ran madly back home. Describes the protagonist's realization and his panicked return home.
  • 乃见妇与其母坐门外墙下 - 乃 means then, 见 means saw, 妇与其母 means his wife and her mother, and 坐门外墙下 means sitting under the wall outside the door. Describes the protagonist's discovery of his wife and her mother.
  • 言适纺倦睡去 - 言 means said, 适 means just now, 纺倦 means tired from spinning, and 睡去 means fell asleep. Describes the wife's explanation of her state.
  • 梦至林野中，迷不能出 - 梦至林野中 means dreamed of being in a forest, and 迷不能出 means became lost and could not get out. Describes the wife's dream.
  • 闻几郎在后唤我，乃霍然醒 - 闻 means heard, 几郎在后唤我 means my husband calling me from behind, and 乃霍然醒 means suddenly woke up. Describes the wife's awakening.
  • 与星所见一一相符 - 与星所见 means with what I saw in the dream, and 一一相符 means exactly matched. Describes the wife's confirmation that her dream matched the protagonist's experience.
  • 盖疲之神不守舍，真阳飞越，遂至离魂 - 盖 means it seems that, 疲之神不守舍 means the spirit of fatigue did not stay in the body, 真阳飞越 means true yang energy flew away, and 遂至离魂 means thus causing the soul to separate. Explains the wife's condition as a result of fatigue and loss of spirit.
  • 魄与形离，是即鬼类 - 魄与形离 means the soul separated from the body, and 是即鬼类 means this is what ghosts are. Describes the wife's condition as a form of ghostliness.
  • 与神识起灭，自生幻象者不同 - 与神识起灭 means different from the rise and fall of consciousness, and 自生幻象者不同 means and the spontaneous generation of illusions. Distinguishes the wife's condition from mere hallucinations.
  • 故人或得而见之 - 故 means therefore, and 人或得而见之 means people may see it. Explains why people can sometimes see such phenomena.
  • 独孤生之梦游，正此类耳 - 独孤生之梦游 means the dream walking of the solitary man, and 正此类耳 means is exactly of this kind. Concludes that the protagonist's experience is similar to the wife's condition.

  • 知其迷路，乃遥呼曰 - This clause introduces the protagonist's realization and his shout to his wife. It must be read as a transition from his journey to his interaction with his wife.
  • 几嫂深夜往何处，迤北更无路，且陷淖中矣 - This phrase is the protagonist's warning to his wife. It must be read as a direct address to his wife, emphasizing the danger she is in.
  • 我不能出，几郎可领我还 - This phrase is the wife's response to her husband. It must be read as a plea for help, emphasizing her inability to escape.
  • 急赴之，已无睹矣 - This clause describes the protagonist's futile attempt to find his wife. It must be read as a continuation of his journey and his realization of the supernatural nature of the event.
  • 知为遇鬼，心惊骨栗，狂奔归家 - This clause describes the protagonist's realization and his panicked return home. It must be read as a continuation of his journey and his realization of the supernatural nature of the event.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 李漱 - A historical or literary figure whose sayings are referenced in the text. The exact identity of Li Shu may be uncertain, but the text is attributed to him.
  • 佃户 - Tenant farmers were a common class in feudal China, often living on land owned by landlords or nobles.
  • 鬼 - Ghosts and spirits are common themes in Chinese folklore and literature, often representing unresolved conflicts or supernatural forces.
  • 社公 - The local deity or spirit of the community is a common figure in Chinese folk religion, often invoked for protection or guidance.
  • 孟子 - Mencius is one of the most influential Confucian philosophers, and his works are frequently cited in classical Chinese texts.
  • 乡邻有斗者，被发缨冠而往救之 - This allusion to the *Mencius* is used to criticize unnecessary or ill-advised intervention in conflicts.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '一夕闻兵仗格斗声，阖家惊砱，登墙视之，无所睹，而战声如故' describes a night of ghostly sounds and the family's reaction. The ambiguity lies in the precise nature of the sounds and the family's perception of them.
  • The complex sentence '彼劫我为质，我亦劫彼为质，互控于社公，社公愦愦，劝以互抵息事，俱不肯伏，故在此决胜负' describes the ghosts' argument with the spirits, their attempt to resolve the conflict through mutual hostage-taking, and the local deity's inability to mediate.
  • The sentence '鬼不干人，人反干鬼，鬼有词矣，非开门揖盗乎' uses a metaphor to criticize the family's interference in the ghosts' dispute, comparing it to inviting trouble.
  • The sentence '孟子有言，乡邻有斗者，被发缨冠而往救之，则惑也，虽闭户可也' alludes to a passage from the *Mencius*, but the context and interpretation may require careful consideration.

Proper Nouns

  • 李漱 - The author or source of the text, whose exact identity may be uncertain.
  • 社公 - The local deity or spirit of the community, often associated with a village or neighborhood.
  • 孟子 - Mencius, the Confucian philosopher whose works are cited in the text.
